description: Apollo 7 Flight Journal - Day 1, part 3: S-IVB Takeover Demonstration, Separation, and First Phasing Maneuver

tokens: The ORDEAL box is an add-on to the spacecraft's guidance system that turns the 8-ball display into a sort of artificial horizon.
The designers of the G&N system intended it to be entirely referenced from an inertial standpoint.
The crew fought for a capability to see their attitude with respect to the ground below and the ORDEAL was the result.
It provides a drive signal to make the 8-ball rotate in pitch at a rate which matches their orbit (orbital rate or orb-rate), whether that be around Earth or the Moon.
Therefore the name ORDEAL - Orbital Rate Display Earth And Lunar.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: It's a happy crew and the toilet jokes are flowing.
Guenter Wendt is the 'pad leader', employed to supervise the crew's entry into the spacecraft at the white room.
Apollo crews were fond of him and appreciated his deep professionalism at the launch pad prior to lift-off.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control Houston at 1 hour, 21 minutes into the flight.
We've just had a little thrill here in the Apollo Control Center in Building 30 in Houston.
We had a rather complete power outage in the building, the first one I can recall in the history of this particular control center.
We didn't lose all power, of course.
Certain critical elements like the computers continued to run on emergency power lines, and emergency power circuits left certain lights, exit lights and that sort of thing lighted in the halls.
But all of our consoles went dead, lost power.
Our lights were out here in the control center except for certain emergency red lights.
I would clock the outage as occurring about 20 minutes after the hour and lasting perhaps 60 to 90 seconds, about a minute - minute and a half duration.
We have verified that all the computers, the critical computers running on line with this mission, continue to run.
They are located on the first floor of this building.
We were not in contact, of course, with the spacecraft at the time, and had we been, we would have continued to be able to go out of the building.

All of our comm circuits continued to work, we were simply without lights, and we didn't know, in other words these comm circuits are set up in such a way that we know whether we are radiating by a flashing white light or we are monitoring when we simply have a steady yellow light.
On each console you have such an array of buttons to various back rooms and to points outside of this building, to the Cape, to other places.
We lost power on all those kinds of circuits, we lost all of our video capability to monitor the various slides and we do not yet have an explanation of just how or why this occurred.
But at 1 hour and 23 minutes into the mission we are back up in good shape with full power.
This is Apollo Control in Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 001:24:23 Huntsville Comm Tech:Huntsville AOS [Acquisition Of Signal].
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 001:24:4l Stafford:Apollo 7, this is Houston through the Huntsville.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston; 1 hour, 25 minutes into the flight.
Capsule Communicator Tom Stafford is putting in a call to the communicator on the ship Huntsville halfway between Hawaii and California.
Let's cut in on that conversation now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Are we transmitting simultaneously and receiving VHF?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Are you going to contact - have you heard Apollo 7?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I read you loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: 001:26:47 Huntsville Comm Tech:Houston, Comm Tech Huntsville.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 001:26:52 Huntsville Comm Tech:Apollo 7 copied you loud and clear.
Apollo 7 copied you loud and clear.

tokens: Go ahead and relay through the Huntsvilie the S-IVB tank pressures; and again just to remind you, call program 47 prior to the LOX dump.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Tank pressures, I'll give you present readings: 24 on A, 24 on B for oxidizers; fuel is 13 on A, and 13 on B.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Huntsville M and O, Houston, CapCom.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: 001:27:17 Huntsville Comm Tech:Go ahead, Huntsville M and O.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We can only copy him when we are S-band locked.
We are presently experiencing trouble with locking two way VHF Band.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger, you can't read any VHF?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: 001:27:34 Huntsville Comm Tech:Negative at this time.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Now reading you loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: That's affirm on both A and B, and I have it logged.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: 001:28:41 StaffordRoger, did you get me transmitting in the blind over the Huntsville, Wally?
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Well, I just - to read the tank pressures and to call program 47 prior to the LOX dump.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I have tank pressures logged for 01 plus 06 plus 15, 01 plus l4 plus 50, if you're ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: We'll contact you over California in a couple of seconds.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston; 1 hour, 31 minutes into the flight.
We should establish contact through Guaymas momentarily.

To recap a bit on that power outage that we experienced about 10 minutes ago, I clocked it at 20 minutes after the hour, the duration was something on the order of 1 to 2 minutes, and initially it was a rather complete power outage.
We still had communications on the circuits, but we did not have the light showing us on which circuits we had communications.
We were not in touch with the spacecraft at the time, and the computers, the critical computers, the on-line computers continued to run through a separate and a secure emergency power source.
Since that time, for about the past 5 minutes, we have been without our TV data displays until such time as additional power could be brought over on that circuit.
Now we have the spacecraft in touch, and let's listen to Tom Stafford and Wally Schirra discuss the situation.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Everything looks good on the IVB back there, and you're Go for the dump.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: And also for the data, they plan to dump the DSE over MILA, and we'll have a real fast evaluation on the voyage.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Looks like Guaymas is working pretty hard down there; we can see it.
Tell me when you get that data on the S-IVB now.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: You might tell Walt, what they did is they rewound the tape recorder over Canary, and if he has any additional voice that he wants to place on, he can place it on there now.
They'll dump it again over MILA.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: They didn't get your remarks on boost because they rewound it over Canary, Walt, per the Flight Plan.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Well, all meter readouts were normal, and I did list then individually on the insertion text, and it's on the tape, and that's about the best we can do.
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: And we're standing by for the dump shortly.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: It's a fantastic world up here.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston; at 1 hour, 34 minutes into the flight.
Apparently there is some discrepancy in our earlier report on the power outage.
I want to absolutely emphasize that there was no affect at all on the computers in Building 30.
They have an emergency power source which cut in and handled that load just perfectly.
We did lose light in the building in the halls, and we lost lights on our console.
We continued to have communications, however; we continued to have phone service.

For a period of about 1 to 2 minutes we were without most of the lights in the building, and we were able to see through an emergency over head system a faint red light which illuminates regardless of - in case of complete power outage.
But again I want to emphasize there was no power outage at all on the computers tracking this mission.
We'll go back now and listen to the events as we move across the States.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're reading your DSKY; looks like you're getting some Delta-V.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The dump appears to be proceeding normally.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: This is Apollo Control Houston with the spacecraft just east of New Orleans and proceeding toward Elean Pass, it will take it almost directly over Savannah, Georgia.
We are 1 hour, 40 minutes into the flight and we have completed at least the initial part of the oxygen dump, and we have now opened the liquid hydrogen vent valves.
They will shortly be closed at the completion of that exercise.
The liquid hydrogen valve is to be closed in 1 hour, 45 minutes into the flight, just east of Florida.
About a minute later, we'll stop the - stop dumping the residual LOX.

Earlier, the booster console operator told me there should be a - on the order of 1 percent residual liquid oxygen in the S-IVB, which remains attached, of course, to the spacecraft; and 1 percent with in terms of weight would be 1,000 to 1,500 lbs [450 to 680 kg].
Very little conversation from the crew as they came across the States in this pass, but we did get a good look at the systems.
We got a cabin that stabilized at about 5.3 psi.
We know the temperature is running a steady 66 degrees [19°C] inside.
Earlier, by the Australia stations, Schirra reported that they're having a ball and he was enthusiastic over the fact that the windows have not clouded at all, this was a recurring problem in Gemini.
He also commented as they moved across Australia, what an advantage it was to watch the booster operation through the center hatch window, one of five windows available.
We are now starting our helium dump from the S-IVB, and again no conversation at all on this eastern portion of the State swing.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: There is a meter on Panel 2 of the Main Display Console which indicates the partial pressure of CO2rather than O2in the cabin atmosphere.
This is likely to be wh Wally is refering to.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Our cabin press now is being - very rapidly it seems to me, we're down to about 5.5.
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: Could you verify that the S-IVB passivisation is complete?
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: The passivation is complete, and you can terminate 47.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: You have our readout on the DSKY?
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: We're waiting for an update on Nav stars for program 52.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: We're working on it right now.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Your stars for your P52 are stars number 2 and stars number 4.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: One of the crew, likely Donn as it is normally the CMP's role, is about to carry out a realignment of the spacecraft's guidance platform.
Two stars are accurately sighted upon, yielding their direction.
The computer can use this information to check how far the platform has drifted out of alignment with the celestial sphere and to bring it back onto perfect alignment.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Here are the numbers; we have trouble pronouncing words ourselves.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Here are the numbers; we have trouble pronouncing the words ourselves.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: We are preparing to jettison the optics covers shortly.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Jack, for the record our Delta-V counter is at 33 feet per second.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Okay, we got it all in the - that was the Delta-V counter?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: 001:54:52 SwigertMILA reports your DSE voice quality on the dump was very good.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Before he can use the spacecraft's sextant and telescope for the first time, Donn must remove the covers on the outside of the hull that protect their external optical surfaces.
He does this by moving one of the optics control levers fully right.
At the same time, he observes the progress of the operation through the telescope eyepiece.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You are 1 minute LOS Canary and we've computed a cabin leak rate.
We find it to be one-half of spec value.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: The optic covers are jettisoned and Donn is tracking them.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Donn is getting a taste of an issue that would visit every CMP.
Unless the spacecraft is in a planet's shadow, any object in the field of view of the optics interferes with the CMP's ability to see the stars.
If the item is large like these optics covers, it dazzles him; if it is small, it confuses him with its star-like appearance.
Unfortunately, when travelling between Earth and the Moon, the spacecraft tends not to be in shadow.
Also, it is regularly emitting substances like water and urine which form ice crystals, and dust particles tend to be shed every time the optics are rotated.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hey, Jack, the SPS propellant tank temperature has held pretty much right around 70, ever since lift-off.
I never did get a feedback on what kind of a duty cycle they expect on those heaters - I haven't turned them On, but for the last couple of hours they are getting by with no heat at all.
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: I couldn't copy that, Walt; you're down very low.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: The SPS tank temps, I think.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: I thought it was the SPS tank temps.
transcript.speaker: Unknown

tokens: 002:12:12 StaffordApollo 7, this is Houston through Tananarive.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger, we're getting a lot of background noise on the HF coming in [garble] here, but you're coming in loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger, you're putting through a lot of echo, and you are just readable.
We just ran through the Orion constellation, and it was very pretty.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Roger, how do the stars look through both the telescope and sextant compared to the simulator?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay, we're going to give you a time hack at 40 minutes to go until separation in about 2 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: And we have a GET for the pitchdown maneuver and the inertial maneuver.
Do you want to copy it?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: 002:13:25 StaffordOkay, GET of pitchdown is 02 plus 42 plus 55; GET of inertial attitude, 02 plus 51 plus 10.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger, pitchdown at 02 plus 42 plus 55, and inertial at 02 plus 51 plus 10.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: We are going to give a 40-minute hack counting down, so you can set your watch.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay, I'm all set here, Tom.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: This is Apollo Control Houston, 2 hours, 15 minutes into the flight.
We have established contact with the crew via the Tananarive station and here's how the conversation is going.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Forty minutes, counting down for Sep.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We have our gyro-torquing angles if you'd like to copy.
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: Roger, DSKY readout follows: this is Verb 06, Noun 93, minus 00012, plus 00023, plus 00186.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: [Garble] In fact, I'm going to get [garble].
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Okay, that's one more time hack.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: What was your star angle difference?
That's the only one we were questioning.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: As part of the procedure for sighting on two stars for the realignment, the computer throws a number called the star angle difference.
The computer knows the angle between the two stars selected for the realignment.
It also knows the angle between the two sightings just made by the CMP.
If the sightings have been made perfectly, the difference between these two angles should be zero.
In this case there was a difference of two hundredths of a degree.
This value was a crude measure of the CMP's accuracy and therefore became a point of pride for many of these competitive ex test pilots.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're going to go on to another...
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Hey, we've got a real nice, clean cabin here, very few little particles floating around.
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: There are a few very small particles, one looks like [garble].
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: You're one minute to LOS Tananarive.
We'll pick you up at Carnarvon in about 8 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Navigating at four balls 1 on the second test.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Donn has carried out the realignment procedure a second time and has achieved a star angle difference of only one hundredth of a degree.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We have Loss Of Signal by Tananarive.
The spacecraft moving across the Indian Ocean.
During the pass across Australia, Carnarvon should acquire about 10 minutes - about 8 minutes from now; 2 hours, 28 minutes into the flight.
We will be set up to decide on whether we will proceed with the crew takeover, the crew flying of the S-IVB.
I understand this configuration we are in right now, we essentially have a spacecraft about 100 feet long.

Consider the Command Module, the Service Module, the SLA panels and the nearly 60-foot-long second stage S-IVB, still hooked up together and the second stage is to be unhooked and jettisoned at 2 hours, 55 minutes into the flight.

That would occur after the crew has gone through an exercise whereby they will get the feel of what the control problems are and just how does it feel to fly such a big kluge from the vantage point of the Command Module.
The - Donn Eisele, the center - the Command Module Pilot, will return to his couch.
He has been down in the Lower Equipment Bay looking over some of his gear and going through some stowage and unstowing activities.
He'll return to his couch for these upcoming activities with the S-IVB leading up to the separation.
When we acquire at Carnarvon, we'll come back to you.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: You won't need a state vector update.
I guess Donn did so good there - and you are Go for your S-IVB takeover.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And Wally, after you get through with the S-IVB control test there, let me know when you arm your Logic and we'll then take a look at it and give you a Go for Pyro Arm.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston; 2 hours, 28 minutes into the flight.
We have established contact with Apollo 7 by Carnarvon, and the crew has been given a Go.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We turned both cabin fans off about an hour ago because the noise is really terrific, and we just put one back on again to circulate some air, out of the noise from both cabin fans is way up there.
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: Well, we've got a dead period here.
Perhaps earlier we had given an indication that the control thrusters during this S-IVB takeover would be thrusters on the Service Module; that's not correct.
They are the thrusters, the small pitch control thrusters on the second stage, the S-IVB itself.
The crew will be operating those from the cabin through logic circuits down through the Instrument Unit which is the principal electronic element joining the booster and the spacecraft.
That event of course is presently programmed for 2 hours and 55 minutes; we are at 2 hours and 30 minutes right now.
We should get some reports on the attitudes as we move across Australia and we'll just leave the line open.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: I'm sorry; we'll wait till you get through with this before we take over here.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: The S-IVB is working very well, and we're now pitching up.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: The Apollo 7 crew is controlling the total spacecraft and booster combination at this time through the thrusters on the S-IVB.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Next will be thrust roll for 5 degrees.
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: There's much cross-coupling with this thing.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: You want to hit your logic down so we can look at that?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: And after Carnarvon here, which we'll lose you in about 2 minutes, we are going to do some remoting through ARIA to get - complete this DTO.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Apollo 7, you're Go for Pyro Arm.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: We can see on the night side, the APS thrusting on the S-IVB.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: As a rule, flight's just like Gemini?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: It's a pretty big blob of light; it's sort of a yellow-orange light.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: When you are dumping some of our tapes, we'll be going live on some of these things to make sure we've got complete coverage.
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: Manual attitude, three of them at Rate Command.
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: That's the [garble] stand by for their on that [garble].
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: TVC servo power, AC 1, main A.
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: Circuit breakers EDS, three of them closed.
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: EDS power, On, and light, On.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: We called for GET to be reset here.
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: 002:36:31 ARIA Comm Tech:ARIA 2 has two-way lock; ARIA 2 has two-way lock.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Read you loud and clear, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: ARIA 2 has us for about 10 minutes here, and then we'll pick up ARIA 3 for about another 10 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: That's a very nice light opera.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Do you think you'll like those ARIA's there?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Okay, Jack, can you verify the tape recorder is in Record Forward, and we'll go to High Bit Rate for the S-IVB maneuver?
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: If we're running through AIRA, you're going to want me to go to High Bit Rate?
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: Apollo 7, EECOM tells me they will control it for Sep.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: You will control it for Sep.
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: The crew has completed the maneuvers using the S-IVB thrusters.
You heard Walt Cunningham going through the countdowns and giving Wally Schirra each of the angles and in essence, amounted to going to 5 degrees in every attitude visual and yaw in these twice.
Wally seemed impressed with the handling characteristics of this nearly 100 foot combination of booster and spacecraft.
We are now remoting through a series of aircraft which are strung out across the - or flying in large square patterns in the southwest Atlantic, southwest Pacific.
We should have continuous comm with the spacecraft up through Hawaii.
Hawaii is to acquire - stand by one here.
Hawaii is to acquire at 2 hours, 53 minutes into the flight.
It'll be then within the Hawaii area of acquisition that we will separate from the S-IVB.
If this were the lunar mission, that is approximately the point where we might very well ignite the S-IVB over the long burn out to the Moon to put us on a lunar trajectory.
At 2 hours, 39 minutes into the flight, this is Apollo Control standing by for any additional comm.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo 7, this is Houston through ARIA 3 - How do you read me?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger, read you loud and clear, Tom.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Apollo 7, this is Houston through ARIA 3.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We read you loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: We can read you about one-by, Wally.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You're now coming in about three-by-three.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Remote - Remote S-band only if it is better.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston; 2 hours, 45 minutes into the flight, and it's unlikely that we will have any additional com during this period, so we will take the circuit down now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 002:46:30 ARIA Comm Tech:ARIA 3, ARIA 3, ARIA contact Remote [garble] Remote VHF...
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo 7, how do you read through ARIA 3?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We have the PAD for the phasing maneuver.
We'll send it up to you or give it to you through Hawaii whenever you're ready for it.
We're not in any hurry, but whenever you're ready for it.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Are we recording high bit rate?
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Did you hear that on the ground?
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: You're saying it was loud, right?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Loudest sound heard round the world.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston; 2 hours, 55 minutes into the flight; and just 30 seconds ago, precisely at 2 hours and 55 minutes, the crew reported they had separated from the S-IVB.
We are in contact over Hawaii, and let's listen for conversation.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: I can see a thruster firing action in daylight.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: This is Apollo Houston, From the ground we know that the spacecraft is pitching over, turning around, and having a good long look.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: I can see little tiny particles out the right-hand window way down; looks like pieces of chaff.
I would assume that came from the separation of the S-IVB.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: I assume that she is still there then.
Tom, we've got same old split [garble] when we pitched out.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Looks like you are going straight in.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Same [garble] It's absolutely beautiful here, and we got a lot of loose particle chaff sitting at about...
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Chaff seems to be oriented mostly between 3 o'clock and 5 o'clock from my point of view here in the right seat and between 9 o'clock and 12 o'clock.

The other two quadrants are relatively clean; and the SLA panel at the top, left, and bottom are opened at - I would guess to he about a 45-degree angle, and the SLA panel on the right is just opened maybe 30 degrees at the very best.
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: Looks like you are looking at a four-jawed angry alligator.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: That is Walt Cunningham giving that report on the position of the SLA panels.
He is in conversation with Tom Stafford who is an expert on angry alligators from the Gemini days.
The crew is simulating a docking approach at this time in to the SLA area.
They won't go in so close as to touch it, but they will operate in the area.
They are taking pictures and in general, they will fly a formation with the S-IVB for the next 10 to 15 minutes.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Go ahead and get the EDS power switch Off if you want to.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Huntsville M and O, this Houston CapCom.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: 003:02:12 Huntsville Comm Tech:Houston, Huntsville M and O, go.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Are we getting through to Apollo 7?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: 003:02:20 Huntsville M and O:We cannot acquire the spacecraft presently with S-band.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston; 3 hours and 2 minutes into the flight.

The ship Huntsville, which is parked between Hawaii and the coast of California, is experiencing some difficulty apparently with patching us through, so our - we do have a voice communication drop out with the spacecraft.
The combination of the California station and/or Guaymas, Mexico station should acquire just any second and we would look for considerable improvement in the communication.
The crew had turned around, they were taking a close look at the large SLA panels, which are - the four of them are opened up.
They are photographing them, and Cunningham reported some chaff.
As yet we haven't been able to identify the source of the chaff in the area.
No problem, but he did report it.
He also reported seeing thruster action in the daylight.
We are now, I believe, attempting to go through Guaymas.
Let's go back to the report.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We have AOS apparently with S-band, experience a drop of signal from handover, Hawaii to Huntsville.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We've got a [garble] out there about a couple or 300 feet.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: You might check your EDS power switch Off, if you want to.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Have you got any kind of update for us for the Sep maneuvers?
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Are you ready to copy it?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Give me a call when you are ready.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: It's a phasing maneuver, 003 20 all balls, Noun 82 N/A, 1641 plus 1224, 00057, 32538, Noun 48 N/A, zero plus 16; and roll, pitch, and yaw are 183, 299, 002; remarks: SEF, heads down, retrograde minus X thrusters.
You should he in your retro-attitude by 3 plus 16 plus 30.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Upgrade for Sep maneuvers, 003, 20, 00, 1641, plus 1224, 00057, down retrograde minus-X thrusters.
You should he in your retro-attitude by 3 plus 16 plus 30.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Understand update for Sep maneuver 003, 20, 00, 1641, check 1224, 00057, 32538.
Noun 48 now on schedule; roll, pitch, and yaw 183, 299, 002.
SEF heads down, retro, and use minus X thrusters.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: I copied, but I didn't get your pitch; but I want to give it to you again.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We expect some nonpropulsive venting up near the front end of the S-IVB between 3 plus 08 and 3 plus 09.
The booster will make a retrograde maneuver at 3 plus 16 plus 55.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Understand nonpropulsive venting between 03:08 and 03:09, and the booster will be retro-venting at 03:16:55.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: That's when the maneuver will be commanded.
You should be able to see it maneuver around.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Confirm that your TVC servo power number 1 is Off.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: There is quite a small-type debris still inside the S-IVB.
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: All the internal structure looks just fine.
There is one set of cords that's running around - one set of cords running around that seems to be going to a panel that didn't open too far.
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston; with the spacecraft almost over the central United States.
We are getting a pretty descriptive view of what the S-IVB looks like, primarily from Walt Cunningham.
He has reported among other things, that one of the four SLA panels is not open completely.
They should be back to nearly 90 degree position, I think one of them is open only a third or a half the way.
I heard a figure earlier of 30 degrees that will have to be confirmed later.
In several times, Cunningham has commented on the venting, the LOX venting from the S-IVB.
Apparently it's a pretty spectacular show.
We will continue to monitor as they move across the states.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We have got - Ponchartrain in the back of the S-IVB.
I can see the bridge right across it.
We should have a - unfortunately, it's too cloudy for us to look at, Tom, but New Orleans looks good.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Understand you can see New Orleans.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We got a shot of the [garble] across the lake cutting about.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: is cloud covered until you get over here, though.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: We're looking right down at the Cape.
We can get a picture of it in the background.
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: You got a picture of them over the Cape in the background.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: You on top of the booster this time, Wally?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: You on top of the booster?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: [Garble] we got some real great stuff here.
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: In about a minute, the booster should start its retrograde maneuver.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The booster is - engine is set up facing down toward the Atlantic Ocean - to straight down.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Got a very slow rate going on the booster.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Except for that one panel, everything looks like it's just as you'd expect it to be on that S-IVB SLA deployment.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: We've got about 3 minutes to go to the phasing maneuver, and are you all set up for the roll, pitch, and yaw?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We've [garble] and roll, and we [garble] attitude shortly.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Our - GNC just confirmed that inertial pitch attitude is 299 degrees.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I'll give you a Mark at 60 seconds: two, one.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: T minus 60 seconds; minus 30 seconds; 10 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: If you are ready on the ground, we are going to start checking our main Reg.
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: You can go ahead and terminate program 47 if you want to.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We are trying to get a few more pictures after we set; we have made the burn one-tenth of a foot per second [garble].
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Tom, we had P47 running there a couple of minutes early, and we picked up about a foot and a half per second in register two.
I guess you can pick that up on your downlink; you might have somebody consider whether they want to re-do the state vector or not.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Look, we're going to have you at Ascension in just a couple of minutes, and we'd like to get a PP02 reading.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Also, what was your closest point of approach, Wally, to the IVB?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I'd say about 4 or 5 feet, Tom.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: We're a little worried to get in - about getting in there with that one cocked panel.
It's probably a good decision to drop those things off.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: This is Apollo Control, Houston; 3 hours, 25 minutes into the flight and we have moved out of the acquisition zone of the Antigua station.
We should acquire Ascension in a very few minutes.
You heard Schirra say that he had pulled up within 4 to 5 feet of the S-IVB and had done a very close inspection of the SLA Panels.
The - 3 of the panels appear to have opened fully, which in the fully deployed position is a 45-degree angle; one of the panels stopped apparently at about 30 degrees.
On later missions we have, or the present thinking is to simply jettison the panels all together so this is sort of a one-time consideration.
We - the reason for the jettisoning of the panels in future missions is a point which has brought the thinking to that conclusion.
It has to do with the exhaust impingement on the panels as the spacecraft looks directly into the hole, or prepares to make the docking maneuver with the Lunar Module.

We will assume that the crew has concluded it's first phasing maneuver of 6.8 foot per second burn which will have a phasing or a shaping affect on the orbit, a very moderate one, and it will move it some distance, I don't just yet know how much distance, away from the S-IVB.
At 3 hours, 27 minutes into the flight; this is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO
